title: DORA — Governance and Board Accountability (Art. 5)
content:
DORA Article 5 establishes the governance framework for ICT risk in financial entities. Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 entered into application on 17 January 2025 and is directly applicable across all 27 EU member states (no national transposition required, unlike NIS2).

The management body (in practice: the board or its equivalent governing body) bears ultimate accountability for ICT risk. Specifically: the management body must define, approve, oversee, and be responsible for the implementation of all arrangements related to the ICT risk management framework. This is not delegable to the CISO; the CISO operates the framework, but the board owns it.

Key obligations under Art. 5:
- Set the financial entity's risk tolerance level for ICT risk in line with its risk appetite
- Approve, oversee, and periodically review the implementation of the ICT business continuity policy and ICT response and recovery plans
- Approve and periodically review the financial entity's ICT internal audit plans, audits, and material modifications
- Allocate and periodically review appropriate budget for ICT and digital operational resilience
- Approve and periodically review the policy on arrangements regarding use of ICT services provided by ICT third-party service providers
- Be duly informed at least annually of ICT risks, ICT-related incidents, and their impact

The Art. 5 'duly informed' requirement is the practical hook for board-level cyber reporting in scope financial entities: at minimum annual ICT risk reporting, typically supplemented by quarterly briefings in mature programmes.

Members of the management body must actively keep up-to-date with sufficient knowledge and skills to understand and assess ICT risk and its impact. ESAs (the European Supervisory Authorities — EBA, EIOPA, ESMA) have signalled that this implies formal cyber-literacy training for board members at scope entities.

Proportionality: smaller and less complex entities benefit from simplified ICT risk management framework requirements per Art. 16, but Art. 5 governance obligations apply in full regardless of size.

Penalty framework: per Art. 50, competent authorities may impose administrative penalties and remedial measures including public statements, cease-and-desist orders, and pecuniary penalties. EBA Q&A 2024 clarified that fines under DORA can stack with sectoral fines under CRD/MiFID II/Solvency II for the same governance failure.

Scope: financial entities defined under Art. 2 — credit institutions, payment institutions, investment firms, insurance and reinsurance undertakings, crypto-asset service providers, central counterparties, central securities depositories, trading venues, trade repositories, managers of alternative investment funds, UCITS management companies, data reporting service providers, credit rating agencies, statutory auditors and audit firms (limited), administrators of critical benchmarks, crowdfunding service providers, securitisation repositories. Plus ICT third-party service providers designated as critical (CTPPs) under Art. 31.

Use this article when discussing board reporting on cyber, CISO reporting lines, ICT budget approval cycles, or the line between management-body accountability and CISO operational responsibility.

title: DORA — ICT Risk Management Framework (Arts. 6–16)
content:
DORA Chapter II (Articles 6–16) defines the ICT risk management framework that all in-scope financial entities must implement. This is the technical-control backbone of DORA.

Art. 6 — ICT risk management framework: requires a sound, comprehensive, and well-documented framework as part of the overall risk management system. It must include strategies, policies, procedures, ICT protocols, and tools that protect all information assets and ICT assets. Reviewed at least annually, and upon the occurrence of major ICT-related incidents.

Art. 7 — ICT systems, protocols, and tools: must be reliable, equipped with sufficient capacity, technologically resilient, suitable for changing requirements, technologically secure, and equipped with mechanisms for confidentiality, integrity, availability, and authenticity of data.

Art. 8 — Identification: identify, classify, and document all ICT-supported business functions, roles, and responsibilities; identify and classify information assets and ICT assets and document their interdependencies; identify all sources of ICT risk on a continuous basis. Risk assessments at least annually and upon any major change in the network and information system infrastructure.

Art. 9 — Protection and prevention: design, procure, and implement ICT security strategies, policies, procedures, ICT protocols, and tools that aim to ensure resilience, continuity, and availability. Specifically requires (a) minimisation of impact through deployment of appropriate ICT security tools, policies, and procedures; (b) network segmentation and segregation; (c) policies that limit physical or logical access to ICT assets only to what is required for legitimate and approved functions and activities; (d) cryptographic protection of data at rest, in transit, and where strictly necessary in use; (e) strong authentication mechanisms.

Art. 10 — Detection: have mechanisms to promptly detect anomalous activities, including ICT network performance issues and ICT-related incidents, and to identify potential material single points of failure. Detection mechanisms must enable multiple layers of control, define alert thresholds and criteria to trigger and initiate ICT-related incident response processes.

Art. 11 — Response and recovery: ICT business continuity policy that is part of the overall operational business continuity policy. Plans must be tested at least yearly and after every substantive change. Includes scenarios such as cyber-attacks, failures of critical third-party providers, supply-chain failures.

Art. 12 — Backup policies and procedures, restoration and recovery procedures: backup of information regularly, restoration testing periodically, and identification of crown-jewel data that needs differential treatment. Backups must be physically and logically segregated from the source ICT systems.

Art. 13 — Learning and evolving: continuous improvement based on lessons from real and simulated incidents.

Art. 14 — Communication: crisis communication plans for both internal and external stakeholders.

Art. 15 — Further harmonisation of ICT risk management tools, methods, processes, and policies: ESAs to develop draft RTS specifying further the elements above. The first set of RTS were published in 2024.

Art. 16 — Simplified ICT risk management framework: applies to smaller and less complex entities (microenterprises, small payment institutions below certain thresholds). Reduced requirements but core obligations remain.

Key practical implications for CISOs: the Art. 9 list of required controls is the most operational — cryptographic protection, segmentation, strong authentication are now legal requirements, not best practices. Art. 12 backup segregation explicitly addresses ransomware: backups must be physically and logically isolated from primary systems, with restoration tested.

Use this article range when discussing the core technical control framework that maps DORA obligations to NIST CSF v2 controls (PR.AC / PR.DS / PR.IP), or when justifying investment in network segmentation, encryption, MFA, immutable backups, or detection tooling to a regulator-facing audience.

title: DORA — Incident Management, Classification, and Reporting (Arts. 17–23)
content:
DORA Chapter III (Articles 17–23) establishes the most operationally consequential DORA requirements for CISOs: incident classification thresholds and regulatory reporting timelines. These obligations are STRICTER than NIS2 reporting and apply to a different scope of entities.

Art. 17 — ICT-related incident management process: financial entities must define, establish, and implement an ICT-related incident management process to detect, manage, and notify ICT-related incidents. Must include early-warning indicators, procedures for identifying, tracking, logging, categorising, and classifying ICT-related incidents according to their priority and severity and according to the criticality of the services impacted.

Art. 18 — Classification of ICT-related incidents: financial entities must classify ICT-related incidents according to the following criteria:
(a) number and/or relevance of clients or financial counterparts affected
(b) duration of the ICT-related incident, including service downtime
(c) geographical spread, in particular if it affects more than two Member States
(d) data losses entailed by the ICT-related incident
(e) criticality of the services affected
(f) economic impact

The Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/1772 of 13 March 2024 specifies the materiality thresholds for each criterion. Examples: a major incident is one that affects more than 10% of clients OR causes service downtime exceeding a defined threshold based on critical services impacted OR generates economic impact above a specified percentage of operating income.

Art. 19 — Reporting of major ICT-related incidents: the three-tier reporting cascade to the relevant competent authority:
- INITIAL NOTIFICATION: within 4 hours of classification as major (or, at the latest, within 24 hours of the financial entity becoming aware of the incident)
- INTERMEDIATE REPORT: within 72 hours of the initial notification (or sooner if status changes materially)
- FINAL REPORT: not later than 1 month after the intermediate report

The 4-hour clock is the most operationally aggressive timeline in EU cyber regulation — STRICTER than NIS2's 24-hour early warning. It requires CISO programmes to have pre-staged classification workflows, on-call legal counsel familiar with the materiality thresholds, and automated reporting templates aligned with the Commission's ITS-prescribed format.

Art. 20 — Harmonisation of the content and templates of the reports: the ESAs developed the ITS that specifies the standard form and report templates. Reports must follow the ITS format; ad-hoc free-text reports will be rejected.

Art. 21 — Centralisation of reporting of major ICT-related incidents: NCAs forward incident reports to ENISA, ECB, and ESAs via a common platform.

Art. 22 — Supervisory feedback: NCAs may issue feedback or guidance based on incident reports, including remediation requirements.

Art. 23 — Significant cyber threats: financial entities may, on a voluntary basis, notify significant cyber threats to NCAs where the threat is relevant to the financial entity.

Penalty exposure: failure to report a major incident within the timelines or providing materially incorrect classification can attract administrative penalties under Art. 50, plus reputational damage from supervisory disclosure (ESAs may publish anonymised aggregate statistics that effectively name pattern-violators).

Use this article range when discussing incident severity classification, regulatory reporting tooling, on-call legal counsel arrangements, or the integration of incident response playbooks with regulatory notification workflows. The 4-hour clock is the single hardest operational requirement most financial-entity CISOs face under any global regime.

title: DORA — Digital Operational Resilience Testing (Arts. 24–27)
content:
DORA Chapter IV (Articles 24–27) requires financial entities to test their digital operational resilience programme, including a specific category of advanced testing (TLPT — threat-led penetration testing) for the largest and most systemically important entities.

Art. 24 — General requirements for the performance of digital operational resilience testing: testing must be performed on critical or important ICT systems and applications at least yearly. The testing programme must include a full range of appropriate tests, such as vulnerability assessments and scans, open source analyses, network security assessments, gap analyses, physical security reviews, questionnaires and scanning software solutions, source code reviews where feasible, scenario-based tests, compatibility testing, performance testing, end-to-end testing, and penetration testing.

Art. 25 — Testing of ICT tools and systems: vulnerabilities identified through testing must be remediated according to a risk-based prioritisation. Material vulnerabilities found in critical or important functions must be remediated promptly.

Art. 26 — Advanced testing of ICT tools, systems, and processes based on TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing): applies to the largest financial entities identified by NCAs based on size, business profile, and overall risk profile. TLPT must be carried out at least every 3 years, performed by external testers (or internal testers under strict conditions specified in the RTS). The threat intelligence input must be tailored, based on real attacker tactics, techniques, and procedures.

TLPT scope: must cover critical or important functions of the financial entity, including underlying ICT systems, processes, and personnel. TLPT must be conducted on live production systems — not test environments — to assess the entity's actual detection and response capabilities. This is a significant uplift vs traditional pentesting and aligns with the TIBER-EU framework that ECB has run as a voluntary programme since 2018.

Art. 27 — Requirements for testers: TLPT testers (external or internal) must meet criteria specified in the RTS. The TIBER-EU framework's tester accreditation is widely expected to be the de-facto standard. Internal testers face additional independence and conflict-of-interest restrictions.

Key practical implications:
- Annual full-spectrum testing for all in-scope entities is a budget line that didn't exist for many financial entities pre-DORA
- TLPT is expensive: industry estimates run €300K–€1.5M per engagement for a major bank, including threat intelligence input, scenario design, red team execution, blue team analysis, and remediation tracking
- Testing on live production systems requires risk acceptance from the management body (Art. 5 hook back to governance)
- Test results, including identified vulnerabilities and remediation plans, must be retained and made available to NCAs upon request

RTS on TLPT (Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/1774) specifies methodology, scope determination, threat intelligence requirements, tester qualifications, and reporting templates. Adopted 13 March 2024 and entered force with the rest of DORA on 17 January 2025.

Use this article range when discussing pentest programmes, red team budgets, TIBER-EU alignment, vulnerability remediation SLAs, or the trade-off between testing on production vs pre-production systems for DORA compliance.

title: DORA — ICT Third-Party Risk (Arts. 28–30)
content:
DORA Chapter V Section I (Articles 28–30) establishes the third-party risk management requirements that have driven the most contract renegotiation activity across the EU financial services industry in 2024–25. Every major SaaS, cloud, and ICT services contract held by a EU bank, insurer, or investment firm has been amended or is being amended to meet DORA obligations.

Art. 28 — General principles: financial entities remain fully responsible for compliance with and discharge of all obligations under DORA and applicable financial services law when they use ICT third-party service providers. The 'outsource the function, not the responsibility' principle is now legally codified.

Financial entities must maintain a strategy on ICT third-party risk including a policy on the use of ICT services supporting critical or important functions. The strategy must address concentration risk — the risk arising from dependence on a single, or a small number of, ICT third-party service providers.

A register of information must be maintained covering all contractual arrangements on the use of ICT services. The register format is prescribed by ITS (Implementing Technical Standard) and must be reported to NCAs at least annually. The register is the operational evidence base for supervisory review.

Pre-contract due diligence is mandatory: financial entities must assess whether the ICT third-party service provider is appropriate, taking into account the criticality of the function, reputation, financial resources, expertise, organisational structure, risk management arrangements, information security, business continuity arrangements, regulatory or legal restrictions.

Art. 29 — Preliminary assessment of ICT concentration risk at entity level: when entering into a contractual arrangement on the use of ICT services supporting critical or important functions, financial entities must consider whether the contract would lead to (a) contracting with an ICT third-party service provider that is not easily substitutable; (b) having in place multiple contractual arrangements with the same ICT third-party service provider or with closely connected ICT third-party service providers.

Art. 30 — Key contractual provisions: contracts for ICT services supporting critical or important functions must include AT LEAST:
(a) a clear and complete description of all functions and ICT services to be provided
(b) the locations, namely the regions or countries, where the contracted or sub-contracted functions and ICT services are to be provided and where data is processed
(c) provisions on availability, authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality
(d) service level descriptions including updates and revisions
(e) the obligation of the ICT third-party service provider to provide assistance at no additional cost, or at a cost determined ex-ante, when an ICT incident related to the ICT service provided occurs
(f) the obligation of the ICT third-party service provider to fully cooperate with the competent authorities and resolution authorities of the financial entity
(g) termination rights and minimum notice periods, in line with the expectations of competent authorities and resolution authorities
(h) the conditions for the participation of ICT third-party service providers in financial entities' security awareness programmes and digital operational resilience training

Additional provisions for contracts supporting critical or important functions (Art. 30(3)): full and unrestricted access, inspection, and audit rights for the financial entity and the NCA; cooperation in TLPT; comprehensive exit strategies enabling the financial entity to transition to an alternative provider or to in-source the function without disruption or quality degradation.

Key practical implications:
- The 'access, inspection, audit' rights have caused friction with hyperscale cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) that historically resisted on-site audit rights — most now offer DORA-aligned pooled audit arrangements
- Exit strategy requirements force financial entities to maintain credible alternatives — concentration risk becomes a board-level metric
- Sub-contracting chains must be transparent: contracts must require notification of intended material sub-contractor changes

Use this article range when discussing vendor contracts, cloud concentration risk, exit strategies, audit rights, or sub-contractor governance for any ICT service supporting a critical or important financial function.
